inference_unit.problem:
The submission contains two separate experimental research problems that motivated the reported work.
First, in the search for higher-temperature superconductors the community had recently progressed from 30-K superconductivity in La–Ba–Cu–O to 90-K in Y–Ba–Cu–O, but many subsequent claims of still higher transition temperatures suffered from instability, poor reproducibility, or turned out not to be bulk superconductivity. This left a substantive gap: there was no reliably reproducible, bulk superconducting oxide with a transition temperature substantially above the 90–93 K range for high-quality rare-earth cuprates, and the field lacked experimentally accessible compositional or processing routes that reproducibly produced higher onset or zero-resistance temperatures. Conventional solid-state sintering methods and the early Tl–Ba–Cu–O syntheses gave sample-to-sample variability and process sensitivities (for example to annealing) that prevented confident identification of bona fide bulk superconductivity at substantially higher temperatures. As a consequence, both the discovery of materials with reliably higher superconducting transition temperatures (Tc) and the experimental verification of their bulk superconducting properties (resistive zero, diamagnetism, quantitative magnetization) were hindered, limiting progress in establishing new superconducting materials and in testing theories about compositional tuning of Tc.
Second, in the mechanical behaviour of covalently bonded crystals (diamond and other hard ceramics), there was a contrasting problem: while some indentation and microscopy studies indicated that limited dislocation motion can occur even at low temperatures, bulk mechanical tests (three-point bend) identified a brittle-to-ductile transition for diamond near 1,500 °C, implying that multiple slip and extensive dislocation-mediated plasticity are unlikely at substantially lower temperatures. This produced an unresolved inconsistency between local-indentation evidence and macroscopic mechanical tests, and a practical need for methods to induce and control dislocation motion (multiple slip) in diamond and related ceramics at homologous temperatures well below those implied by bulk tests. Without such methods it was difficult to prepare specimens with controlled dislocation densities for electron microscopy and X-ray topography, to study microplasticity systematically, or to assess the role of microplasticity in the performance of crystalline engineering ceramics under service-like contact pressures.

conclusion.title: Observation of bulk superconductivity in Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O with onset ~120 K and zero resistance above 100 K

conclusions.conclusion:
Bulk superconductivity was observed in samples of the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O system: the electrical resistance of typical samples begins to drop sharply at approximately 120 K (onset), the transition midpoint is at approximately 110 K, and zero electrical resistance is achieved above 100 K (example values for a furnace-cooled $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ sample: transition midpoint 112 K and zero-resistance temperature 103 K). The superconducting transition is broad (typically >20 K) in these polycrystalline pellets.
In the empirical search for higher-Tc oxide superconductors, a critical requirement was unambiguous demonstration of bulk superconductivity (zero resistance and bulk diamagnetism) at temperatures exceeding those of well-established rare-earth cuprates. Prior reports of very-high-temperature transitions were frequently unstable or not reproducible and often lacked robust bulk measurements, so it remained unclear whether particular compositional substitutions (for example involving thallium and calcium/barium) could yield reliably higher Tc values. A direct resistive demonstration of zero resistance together with reproducible onset and midpoint temperatures is necessary to establish a new superconducting compound family and to enable subsequent magnetic and compositional characterization; without such evidence the claimed high transition temperatures could not be accepted as bona fide bulk superconductivity.
Fig. 1

conclusion.title: Synthesis procedure for Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O employing BaCu3O4 melt-solid reaction and rapid heat treatment

conclusions.conclusion:
A reproducible sample preparation procedure for Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O materials was established: stoichiometric mixtures of $Tl_{2}O_{3}$, CaO and $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ were mixed, pressed into pellets (typical diameter 7 mm, thickness 2–3 mm), placed into a tube furnace preheated to $880$–$910^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, heated in flowing oxygen for $3$–$5$ minutes and then quenched to room temperature in air; selected specimens were furnace-cooled or subsequently annealed at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in flowing oxygen for several hours. The use of $BaCu_{3}O_{4}$ as a starting material — because it has the lowest melting point among the Ba–Cu oxides and the molten phase is extremely fluid — was adopted to promote melt–solid reactions that are believed to produce more complete reaction than typical solid-state sintering.
Reproducible synthesis routes were missing for candidate high-Tc thallium-containing cuprates: conventional solid-state sintering and other oxide precursors can give incomplete reaction, inhomogeneous phases and strong preparation dependence, which in turn produce sample-to-sample variability in transport and magnetic properties. A synthesis method that provides rapid, reproducible reactions and homogeneity — for example by exploiting a low-melting Ba–Cu oxide to produce transient molten phases for better chemical mixing — was needed so that observed superconducting transitions could be reliably attributed to the intended compound chemistry rather than to uncontrolled inhomogeneity or minority phases. Without such a controlled processing protocol quantitative comparisons of transition temperatures and stoichiometry effects were unreliable.

conclusion.title: Qualitative magnetic evidence for superconductivity: levitation and a.c. susceptibility behaviour

conclusions.conclusion:
Qualitative magnetic measurements indicate superconducting diamagnetism in Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples: a specimen of nominal composition $Tl_{2}Ca_{2}BaCu_{3}O_{9+x}$ levitates above a magnetic field of approximately $5{,}000$ G after immersion in liquid nitrogen, and a $Tl_{2}Ca_{1.5}BaCu_{3}O_{8.5+x}$ sample shows a temperature-dependent a.c. susceptibility with an onset of diamagnetic response that occurs somewhat below the initial resistive drop in the same material (a behaviour previously observed in non-stoichiometric rare-earth superconductors). Quantitative d.c. magnetization measurements were stated to be in progress.
To confirm that resistive transitions correspond to bulk superconductivity it is necessary to demonstrate bulk diamagnetism and to measure magnetic properties quantitatively. Prior to this work many high-temperature transition claims lacked clear magnetic corroboration (levitation, susceptibility, magnetization), leaving open the possibility that resistive anomalies arose from filamentary or surface phases. Simple qualitative magnetic signatures (levitation, a.c. susceptibility onset) are therefore required as initial evidence that the observed resistive zero corresponds to macroscopic superconductivity; subsequent d.c. magnetization is needed to quantify the superconducting volume fraction and to rule out percolative artefacts.
Fig. 2; Fig. 3

conclusion.title: Dependence of superconductivity on annealing and Ca/Ba ratio; semiconducting behaviour in Tl–Ca–Cu–O

conclusions.conclusion:
The superconducting behaviour of Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples depends on processing and composition: annealing at $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in flowing oxygen slightly improves superconductivity in Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O samples (whereas the same anneal depresses or destroys superconductivity in Tl–Ba–Cu–O samples); variation of the Ca/Ba ratio influences superconductivity; and related Tl–Ca–Cu–O compositions prepared under similar conditions exhibit semiconducting temperature dependence down to liquid-nitrogen temperature. Experiments to identify an optimum Ca/Ba ratio were reported to be underway.
Even when resistive and magnetic signatures of superconductivity are observed, understanding and optimizing the phenomenon requires knowledge of how thermal treatment and stoichiometry control the superconducting phase. Prior practice showed strong processing sensitivity in thallium- and barium-containing cuprates, and some compositional substitutions in related cuprates either suppress or enhance Tc. Without systematic observations of annealing effects and composition dependence it is not possible to tune materials to higher Tc, to improve transition sharpness, or to discriminate between intrinsic behaviour and processing-induced artefacts; therefore empirical mapping of anneal treatments and Ca/Ba ratios is necessary for materials optimization.

conclusion.title: Demonstration that multiple slip can occur in diamond under point-loading at 1,000 °C and nominal contact pressure ~10 GPa

conclusions.conclusion:
Multiple slip (extensive dislocation movement) can be induced in diamond by the pressure transmitted through point-loading using a softer indenter material (a cubic boron nitride cone) at a temperature of $1{,}000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and under a nominal contact pressure of about $10\ \mathrm{GPa}$ (title-stated conditions). This shows that substantial dislocation activity in diamond can occur at homologous temperatures below previously emphasized values derived from three-point bend tests.
The mechanical community faced an inconsistency: local-indentation and microscopy observations suggested some dislocation mobility in hard crystals at modest temperatures, whereas macroscopic mechanical tests implied that diamond required temperatures around $1{,}500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to access ductile, multiple-slip behaviour. Without an experimental protocol to generate and observe multiple slip in diamond at lower homologous temperatures, it was not possible to reconcile local and bulk observations, to prepare specimens with controlled dislocation structures for transmission electron microscopy or X-ray topography, or to assess the microplastic contribution to contact-induced deformation under technologically relevant pressures. Demonstrating multiple slip under point-contact loading at $1{,}000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ directly addresses this gap by providing evidence that dislocation-mediated plasticity can be activated under certain contact conditions well below the previously cited three-point bend threshold.

conclusion.title: Methodological route to prepare specimens with controlled dislocation densities at low homologous temperature

conclusions.conclusion:
The point-loading technique using a softer, high-pressure indenter (cubic boron nitride cone) at elevated temperature provides a practical specimen-preparation method that introduces a controlled density of dislocations into diamond (and related hard crystals) at homologous temperatures below $0.3\,T_{m}$ (where $T_{m}$ is the melting point), enabling samples suitable for electron microscopy and X-ray topography.
Systematic microstructural studies of dislocation networks and slip in diamond require specimen preparation routes that reproducibly introduce dislocations at controlled densities and at temperatures low enough to preserve other microstructural features. Previously available methods either produced uncontrolled damage (catastrophic fracture) or required much higher homologous temperatures, limiting the kinds of microstructural investigations that could be performed. A technique that transfers high contact pressure from a softer indenter material to the hard crystal surface offers a way to stimulate dislocation motion and to create microstructures suited for microscopy and X-ray topography without reaching the very high temperatures implied by bulk ductility tests.

conclusion.title: Conceptual implication: microplasticity matters for performance of crystalline engineering ceramics

conclusions.conclusion:
The experimental observation that multiple slip can be induced in diamond under contact pressures at $1{,}000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ emphasizes that microplasticity (dislocation-mediated deformation at microstructural scales) can play a significant role in the contact deformation and mechanical performance of crystalline engineering ceramics and must be considered in their application and performance analyses.
Engineering practice and modelling of hard, covalent ceramics often assume brittle fracture-dominated response at low homologous temperatures, neglecting microplastic mechanisms. However, surface contact pressures and localized thermal conditions encountered in applications can activate dislocation motion even when bulk measurements suggest brittle behaviour. Without acknowledging and quantifying this microplastic contribution, predictions of wear, contact damage, and component lifetime can be incomplete or misleading. Demonstrating that multiple slip is possible under realistic contact conditions motivates a re-evaluation of mechanical performance models to include microplasticity.

inference_unit.open_question:
The authors explicitly acknowledge and leave open several experimental and characterization issues.
For the Tl–Ca/Ba–Cu–O superconductors: (1) quantitative d.c. magnetization measurements were still in progress and therefore the superconducting volume fraction, critical fields and other magnetic parameters remained to be determined; (2) the superconducting transitions are rather broad (typically >20 K), indicating that improved preparation could narrow the transition and possibly raise the zero-resistance temperature, but no optimized processing protocol or its limits were yet established; and (3) the dependence of superconducting behaviour on composition — in particular the optimum Ca/Ba ratio for maximizing transition temperature and sharpening the transition — was not yet determined and experiments to find an optimum ratio were reported as underway. These open points leave unresolved the quantitative magnetic confirmation, the reproducibility bounds, and the compositional optimization necessary to fully establish the materials as reliable, bulk, high-Tc superconductors.
For the diamond/contact-deformation work: an inconsistency in the literature remained between indentation-derived thresholds (suggesting onset of dislocation activity at temperatures below about $1{,}100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) and macroscopic three-point bend-derived brittle-to-ductile transition near $1{,}500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. While the present point-loading result demonstrates multiple slip at $1{,}000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ under specified contact conditions, the broader parameter space (for example, precise ranges of contact pressure, indenter material and geometry, strain rates, and resulting dislocation densities) and how these conditions map onto macroscopic ductility thresholds remain to be systematically quantified. In other words, the temperature, pressure and loading-mode dependence of the onset of multiple slip in diamond and the quantitative control of dislocation density produced by the method are left as unresolved experimental questions.
